title: How to Test If Your VPN Is Working in 2026: IP Leaks, DNS Leaks, and WebRTC Leaks Explained
description: How to test if your VPN is working correctly in 2026: check for IP leaks, DNS leaks, and WebRTC leaks. Step-by-step tests with free tools and what to do if you find a leak.
content: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org","@type":"FAQPage","mainEntity":[{"@type":"Question","name":"How do I know if my VPN is working?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"The simplest test: check your IP address before and after connecting to the VPN. Step 1: disconnect from the VPN and go to ipleak.net or whatismyipaddress.com. Note your real IP address and location. Step 2: connect to the VPN. Step 3: reload ipleak.net. If the VPN is working, the IP address shown should be different from your real IP and should match the VPN server location you connected to. If you connected to a US server, the IP should appear to be in the US. If the IP shown is still your real IP: the VPN connection failed or the software has a bug. Reconnect and test again. A passing IP test confirms the VPN is routing your internet traffic through the VPN tunnel. However, a passing IP test does not guarantee there are no DNS leaks or WebRTC leaks -- those require separate tests."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"What is a DNS leak and how do I test for it?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer

text":"A DNS leak occurs when your device sends DNS queries (the lookups that translate domain names like google.com to IP addresses) outside the VPN tunnel, to your ISP's DNS servers instead of the VPN's DNS servers. Even though your traffic goes through the VPN, your DNS queries reveal which websites you are visiting to your ISP. How to test for DNS leaks: go to dnsleaktest.com or ipleak.net while connected to your VPN. Run the standard or extended test. The results show which DNS servers are handling your queries. If the DNS servers shown belong to your ISP or a third party unrelated to your VPN provider: you have a DNS leak. If the DNS servers shown belong to your VPN provider or a VPN-associated service: no leak. How to fix a DNS leak: most reputable VPN apps (NordVPN, ExpressVPN, Mullvad) have built-in DNS leak protection that routes DNS queries through their own servers. Enable this setting if it is not on by default. On Windows, you can also manually set your DNS servers to your VPN provider's DNS addresses. Mullvad has a DNS leak test tool at mullvad.net/check that also tests for WebRTC leaks in one step."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"What is a WebRTC leak and does my VPN protect against it?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) is a browser technology that enables audio and video calls in the browser (used by Google Meet, Discord web, and similar). WebRTC can reveal your real IP address to websites even when you are connected to a VPN, because it uses a mechanism (STUN servers) that bypasses the VPN tunnel to discover your true network address. This is a browser-level leak, not a VPN-level failure -- the VPN is working, but the browser is leaking your real IP through a separate channel. How to test for WebRTC leaks: visit browserleaks.com/webrtc while connected to your VPN. If your real IP appears under local IP candidates: you have a WebRTC leak. How to fix WebRTC leaks: Firefox: go to about:config, search for media.peerconnection.enabled, set it to false. Chrome: install the uBlock Origin extension (enable WebRTC leak prevention in settings) or the WebRTC Network Limiter extension. Most VPN browser extensions (NordVPN, ExpressVPN, Mullvad all have them) automatically block WebRTC leaks when the extension is active. The VPN desktop app alone does not fix WebRTC leaks -- you need the browser extension or browser setting change."}}]}

Installing a VPN and seeing a green checkmark in the app does not guarantee your traffic is actually private. Here are the tests that confirm your VPN is doing its job -- and what to do when it is not.

The Basic IP Test: 30 Seconds to Confirm the VPN Is On

The fastest test: visit ipleak.net before and after connecting to the VPN. Without the VPN, you will see your real IP address and your approximate location (usually accurate to your city). With the VPN connected, you should see the VPN server's IP address and that server's location. If you connected to a server in Germany, the location shown should be Germany. If you still see your real IP address after connecting: your VPN connection failed silently. Disconnect and reconnect, or try a different server. Some VPN apps have a known issue where they show as connected in the app UI but the tunnel is not actually established -- the IP test catches this immediately.

DNS Leak Test: The Leak Most People Miss

The IP test passing does not mean your DNS queries are private. DNS queries (translating domain names to IP addresses) happen constantly as you browse and can leak outside the VPN tunnel to your ISP's DNS servers. This reveals your browsing activity to your ISP even when your traffic is encrypted. Test at dnsleaktest.com: run the extended test while connected to your VPN. The results show which DNS servers are processing your queries. You want to see servers belonging to your VPN provider, not your ISP. NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad all include DNS leak protection by default -- their apps route DNS through their own servers. If you are using a manual OpenVPN or WireGuard setup without a full VPN app, DNS leak protection may not be automatic. Set your DNS manually to your VPN provider's DNS addresses.

WebRTC Leak Test: The Browser-Level Problem

WebRTC is the most commonly missed leak type. Browser-based video and audio (Google Meet, Discord web, browser games) uses WebRTC, which can expose your real IP directly to websites through a mechanism that bypasses the VPN. Test at browserleaks.com/webrtc: if you see your real IP under any category while the VPN is connected, you have a WebRTC leak. The fix is browser-level, not VPN-level: the VPN desktop app cannot stop WebRTC leaks by itself. Use your VPN provider's browser extension (most major providers have one), which typically includes WebRTC blocking. In Firefox, you can disable WebRTC entirely via about:config (media.peerconnection.enabled = false). In Chrome, WebRTC cannot be fully disabled but can be restricted with extensions like uBlock Origin with WebRTC blocking enabled.

Kill Switch: What Happens When the VPN Disconnects

VPNs sometimes disconnect unexpectedly -- server issues, network changes, sleep/wake cycles. If your VPN disconnects without a kill switch, your traffic briefly uses your real IP until the VPN reconnects. A kill switch blocks all internet traffic if the VPN connection drops, preventing any accidental exposure. Most major VPN providers include a kill switch option in settings -- enable it if you care about continuous privacy. To test if your kill switch works: connect to the VPN, enable the kill switch, then manually disconnect the VPN in the app. Your internet should stop working immediately. When you reconnect the VPN, internet access should resume. If internet access continues when you manually disconnect the VPN with the kill switch on, the kill switch is not working correctly.
